aliases: Papaya and Snow Fungus Tong Sui

english: Papaya and Snow Fungus Tong Sui

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for White Fungus with Papaya Soup. Mapped to generic soup food product as closest defensible anchor. Tremella fuciformis (white fungus) and Carica papaya are the primary ingredients.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Soups and broths) used as closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Chinese sweet fungus soups in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this traditional Chinese sweet soup preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one bowl), providing around 135 kcal, 32.4 g carbohydrate, and 3.6 g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Papaya, raw (FDC ID 169926) and Mushrooms, white, raw (FDC ID 169251); Singapore HPB Foodo — Sweet soup reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for White Fungus with Papaya Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for raw papaya (FDC ID 169926) and white mushroom/fungus (FDC ID 169251) combined with rock sugar contribution, weighted to reflect a typical preparation ratio of approximately 50% papaya, 10% rehydrated white fungus, 35% liquid, and 5% rock sugar. Singapore HPB Foodo sweet soup reference values used to cross-check energy and sugar estimates. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained with source attribution updated to approved sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fruit-based and fungus-based sweet soups — analogous estimate based on papaya GI (GI ~60) and soluble fibre attenuation from Tremella fuciformis polysaccharides; reference: Atkinson FS et al. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for White Fungus with Papaya Soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) raw papaya GI ~60 per Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables; (2) significant attenuation expected from the high soluble polysaccharide content of Tremella fuciformis (white fungus), which slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption; (3) dilution effect of the soup liquid matrix. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 32.4) / 100 = 14.6 g. This is consistent with the input record's estimated GI of 45 and is classified as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g); high potassium from papaya (95 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: White Fungus with Papaya Soup is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and meaningful potassium contribution from papaya, which supports healthy blood pressure regulation; however, portion size should be monitored to limit total sugar intake from added rock sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added rock sugar raises total sugar to 8.5 g/100 g; mitigated by soluble fibre from white fungus (GI ~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this soup in moderate portions (150–200 g) as the low GI (~45) and soluble polysaccharide fibre from Tremella fuciformis help attenuate postprandial glucose response, but the added rock sugar should be minimised or substituted with a low-calorie sweetener to reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low sodium; antioxidant vitamins C and A from papaya
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This soup is cardioprotective in profile — it is virtually fat-free, cholesterol-free, and very low in sodium, while providing antioxidant nutrients (vitamin C 15.5 mg/100 g, vitamin A 45 mcg RAE/100 g) from papaya that support vascular health; it is suitable as a regular dessert for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when prepared with minimal added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C and beta-carotene from papaya; immunomodulatory polysaccharides from Tremella fuciformis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Papaya's high vitamin C content supports collagen synthesis and may reduce oxidative stress in joints, while Tremella fuciformis polysaccharides have demonstrated immunomodulatory properties in vitro; this soup is generally well-tolerated and may be included freely in an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Fungus with Papaya Soup (木瓜燉銀耳 / 木瓜炖银耳, Mùguā dùn yín'ěr) is a traditional Chinese sweet soup (tong sui) originating from Southern China and widely popular in Hong Kong and the broader Cantonese-speaking world. The dish is prepared by simmering rehydrated white fungus (Tremella fuciformis, also called snow fungus or silver ear fungus) with ripe papaya chunks, rock sugar, and optionally red dates (jujube) and goji berries in water or a light broth, typically using a slow double-boiling method to preserve the delicate gelatinous texture of the fungus. The resulting soup is light, subtly sweet, and slightly viscous, with a translucent appearance and a soft, jelly-like mouthfeel from the fungus. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie (approximately 45 kcal/100 g), low-fat, low-sodium dessert with a moderate carbohydrate content (10.8 g/100 g) primarily from natural fruit sugars and added rock sugar. The white fungus contributes soluble polysaccharides that lower the glycaemic index to approximately 45 (low GI), making it more blood-sugar-friendly than many other sweet soups. Papaya provides meaningful vitamin C (15.5 mg/100 g), vitamin A (45 mcg RAE/100 g), and potassium (95 mg/100 g). In Traditional Chinese Medicine, white fungus is prized for nourishing the lungs, moistening the skin, and acting as a natural collagen booster. Clinically, the soup is suitable for individuals with hypertension (very low sodium, potassium-rich), cardiovascular disease (fat-free, cholesterol-free), and Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (low GI, high soluble fibre), with the caveat that added rock sugar should be minimised for diabetic and low-sugar diets. It is served warm in winter and chilled in summer, and is appropriate as a snack or post-dinner dessert for all age groups.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for White Fungus with Papaya Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for papaya and white fungus, cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo sweet soup reference values. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this component-based estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on papaya GI from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, attenuated for white fungus soluble fibre content. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (papaya FDC ID 169926; white mushroom/fungus FDC ID 169251) weighted to a typical preparation ratio, cross-checked with Singapore HPB Foodo sweet soup references. No exact approved-source composite entry exists for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite sweet soup is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the prepared soup as a composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are confirmed zero / naturally zero for this plant-based, fungus-and-fruit sweet soup with no animal-derived ingredients; Tremella fuciformis does not contain meaningful vitamin B12 or vitamin D per USDA FoodData Central mushroom data. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero given the negligible total fat content (0.1 g/100 g) of this plant-based soup. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based vegan preparation. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 300 g entries); only one default serving of 300 g is retained. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Chinese sweet fungus soups. 7. This dish is not a Singaporean dish; therefore Malay, Tamil, and Hindi multilingual name fields are not applicable and are set to null. The dish is Chinese in origin and the mandatory Chinese-script forms are present.
